title: Consumer & producer surplus
summary:
## Measuring welfare in a market

**Consumer surplus** and **producer surplus** measure the *welfare* (net benefit) that buyers and sellers gain from trading at the market price. Together they let us judge how much a market benefits society — and how taxes, subsidies and price controls change that.

## Consumer surplus

**Consumer surplus** = the difference between the price consumers are **willing to pay** (shown by the demand curve) and the price they **actually pay** (the market price).

- On a diagram it is the **area below the demand curve and above the market price**, up to the quantity traded.
- It exists because the demand curve shows that earlier units were valued *more* than the price paid.

## Producer surplus

**Producer surplus** = the difference between the price producers **actually receive** and the **minimum** they would have been willing to accept (shown by the supply curve).

- On a diagram it is the **area above the supply curve and below the market price**, up to the quantity traded.

## Total welfare

**Community/total surplus = consumer surplus + producer surplus.** At the free-market equilibrium this is **maximised**, which is why competitive equilibrium is *allocatively efficient* (no deadweight loss). Anything that moves the market away from equilibrium (a tax, price floor, monopoly) creates a **deadweight welfare loss** — surplus that disappears.

## How surpluses change

- A **fall in price** (e.g. supply increases) → consumer surplus **rises**.
- A **rise in price** → consumer surplus **falls**, producer surplus may rise.
- **Elasticity matters:** with **inelastic demand**, consumer surplus is large and consumers bear more of a tax; the split of surplus between buyers and sellers depends on the relative elasticities of demand and supply.

## Applications

- **Taxes/subsidies:** an indirect tax shrinks both surpluses and creates a deadweight loss; the **incidence** (who loses more surplus) depends on relative elasticities.
- **Price controls:** a maximum price can raise consumer surplus on units still traded but causes shortages; a minimum price can raise producer surplus but causes surpluses.
- **Monopoly:** raises price and restricts output, transferring consumer surplus to producers and creating deadweight loss (A2).

## Why it matters

Surplus analysis is the bridge from positive demand-and-supply mechanics to **normative welfare judgements**. It is the toolkit you use to evaluate every intervention in Unit 3 and beyond.

keyTerms.definition: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay; the area below demand and above price.
The difference between the price producers receive and the minimum they would accept; the area above supply and below price.
Consumer surplus plus producer surplus; maximised at competitive equilibrium.
When resources are allocated to maximise total welfare (price = marginal cost), with no deadweight loss.
The loss of total surplus when output is pushed away from the efficient equilibrium.
The net benefit society gains from the consumption and production of goods.

diagrams: **Surplus diagram:** standard D/S with equilibrium P* and Q*. Shade the triangle below D and above P* (consumer surplus) and the triangle above S and below P* (producer surplus).
**Effect of a price fall:** show supply shifting right, price falling, and the consumer-surplus triangle enlarging.
**Deadweight loss:** show an indirect tax shifting supply up; mark the smaller surpluses and the deadweight-loss triangle between the old and new quantities.
tips: Get the **areas right**: consumer surplus is *below demand, above price*; producer surplus is *above supply, below price*. Mislabelling loses marks.
State that **total surplus is maximised at competitive equilibrium** to explain allocative efficiency.
Link surplus changes to **elasticity** — who gains/loses more depends on relative elasticities of demand and supply.
Use surplus diagrams to **evaluate interventions** (taxes, price controls, monopoly) and identify **deadweight loss**.
Always **shade and label** the relevant areas on the diagram rather than just describing them in words.
